query: IAEA Zaporizhzhya nuclear power plant off-site power line status June 2026 DG Update
answer:
## IAEA Zaporizhzhya Nuclear Power Plant: Off-Site Power Line Status — June 2026

### Executive Summary

As of early June 2026, the Zaporizhzhya Nuclear Power Plant (ZNPP) — Europe's largest nuclear facility — remains critically dependent on a **single 750 kV main power line** (the Dniprovska line running northward). A **330 kV backup line** was restored in February–March 2026 during an IAEA-brokered ceasefire but operates with "reduced independence." On **June 3, 2026**, the plant suffered its **17th complete loss of off-site power** since Russia's full-scale invasion, triggered by a drone strike on the Nikopol substation. Emergency diesel generators (EDGs) powered the plant for approximately 20 minutes before external power was restored.

---

### Current Power Line Configuration

| Power Line | Voltage | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dniprovska line (northward)** | 750 kV | **Operational — sole main line** | Runs north across former Kakhovka Reservoir to Dniprovska substation. Repeatedly interrupted by substation attacks. |
| Kakhovska line (southwest) | 750 kV | **Destroyed** | Commissioned 2021; destroyed during conflict |
| Pivdennodonbaska line (southeast) | 750 kV | **Destroyed** | Ran to Donetsk Oblast |
| Zaporizka line (east/north) | 750 kV | **Destroyed** | Ran to Zaporizhzhia Oblast |
| **Thermal power station backup line** | 330 kV | **Restored with limitations** | Repaired Feb–March 2026 during IAEA-brokered ceasefire; "reduced independence" per IAEA |

**Bottom line:** 1 of 4 original 750 kV lines operational; 1 backup 330 kV line restored with reduced capability. The plant is effectively dependent on a single transmission corridor.

---

### June 2026 Key Events

- **June 1, 2026:** A drone strike damaged a turbine building at ZNPP (NucNet).
- **June 3, 2026 — 17th Blackout:** A drone strike on the **Nikopol substation** disconnected the sole remaining power line for ~20 minutes. EDGs activated automatically. Power was restored shortly before midnight. IAEA DG Rafael Grossi warned the incident "highlights continuing risks to nuclear safety during the conflict."
- **June 5, 2026:** IAEA Board meeting scheduled, at which Grossi was expected to address ongoing nuclear safety concerns in Ukraine.

---

### 2026 Chronology of Off-Site Power Events

| Date | Event | Key Detail |
|---|---|---|
| Jan 17 | Ceasefire for repairs | 5th IAEA-brokered local truce for power line work |
| Jan 30 | IAEA Board extraordinary meeting | Netherlands raised "grave concerns"; 12 members requested written safety assessment |
| Feb 6 | Grid attack causes reactor shutdown | Operating Ukrainian NPP auto-disconnected from grid |
| Feb 19 | Single main line confirmed | IAEA reported ZNPP on "sole remaining main power line" |
| Feb 27 | Ceasefire and repairs begin | Demining, switchyard component replacement underway |
| ~Early Mar | 330 kV backup line restored | Repaired during ceasefire; limited capability |
| Mar 20 | ZNPP relies on backup for hours | Main 750 kV line under maintenance; 12 total power losses at this point |
| Mar 24 | Main 750 kV line lost | Dniprovska line disconnected |
| Apr 16 | 14th total power loss | All off-site power lost; EDGs activated |
| Apr 30 | 15th power loss | External power lost again |
| May 29 | 16th power loss | EDGs activated |
| Jun 3 | **17th power loss** | Drone strike on Nikopol substation; 20-min outage |

---

### Backup Diesel Generator Status

Emergency diesel generators have been activated during **all 17 blackout events**. The IAEA has repeatedly emphasized that EDGs are a **last-resort backup**, not a sustainable power supply. Their operation depends on continued fuel deliveries — a logistical vulnerability in an active conflict zone.

---

### IAEA Safety Pillar 4 Assessments

**Pillar 4** of Grossi's Seven Indispensable Pillars states: *"There must be a secure off-site power supply from the grid for all nuclear sites."*

Key DG assessments in 2026:
- **Jan 30:** Grossi told the Board there is a "clear deterioration in Ukraine's energy grid since the agency began monitoring it in 2024."
- **Feb 6:** Called grid events "a stark reminder of the ever-present risks to nuclear safety."
- **Mar 20:** Warned "the ZNPP's fragility in the face of limited off-site power options is putting constraints on electrical maintenance."
- **Jun 3:** Stated the 17th blackout "highlights continuing risks to nuclear safety during the conflict."

---

### Additional Context

- **All six reactors remain shut down** (five in cold shutdown, one historically in hot shutdown for steam/heating).
- **Reactor restart is not feasible:** Three of four 750 kV lines are destroyed and the Kakhovka Reservoir (destroyed June 2023) is unavailable for cooling.
- **Russian licensing:** Rostekhnadzor issued 10-year operating licenses for Units 1 and 2 (Dec 2025 and Feb 2026 respectively).
- **IAEA continuous presence** since September 2022.

key_facts: As of June 3, 2026, ZNPP has only 1 of 4 original 750 kV off-site power lines operational (the Dniprovska line running northward).
A 330 kV backup line was restored in February-March 2026 during an IAEA-brokered ceasefire but operates with 'reduced independence' per the IAEA.
On June 3, 2026, ZNPP suffered its 17th complete loss of off-site power since the full-scale Russian invasion, caused by a drone strike on the Nikopol substation.
The June 3 outage lasted approximately 20 minutes; emergency diesel generators powered the plant until external power was restored before midnight.
A drone strike on June 1, 2026 damaged a turbine building at ZNPP.
IAEA DG Grossi warned the June 3 blackout 'highlights continuing risks to nuclear safety during the conflict.'
All six VVER-1000 reactor units remain shut down (five cold, one historically hot for steam production).
IAEA has brokered at least 5 local ceasefires near ZNPP to enable power infrastructure repairs.
Grossi reported a 'clear deterioration in Ukraine's energy grid since the agency began monitoring it in 2024' at the January 30, 2026 Board meeting.
Reactor restart is not feasible due to loss of 3 of 4 main power lines and destruction of Kakhovka Reservoir (June 2023).
Emergency diesel generators have been activated during all 17 blackout events since the war began.
Russia's Rostekhnadzor issued 10-year operating licenses for ZNPP Units 1 (Dec 2025) and 2 (Feb 2026).
